summary: Exponent rules let you combine powers of the same base by adding, subtracting, or multiplying exponents instead of expanding them.
explanation: When multiplying powers with the same base, add the exponents; when dividing, subtract them. When raising a power to another power, multiply the exponents. Any nonzero number raised to the 0 power equals 1, and a negative exponent means "take the reciprocal": \(a^{-n} = 1/a^n\). These rules only combine directly when the bases match — \(2^3 \times 3^2\) cannot be simplified using the product rule since the bases differ. A common GRE trap is applying exponent rules to addition instead of multiplication (e.g. \(a^m + a^n \neq a^{m+n}\)).
